title: Visual Analytics and Applications
text:
Hello I am Tran Thi Thanh Van, you can call me Haley.
Welcome to ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications homepage.
In this website, you will find my coursework. Materials here are based on https://isss608-ay2025-26apr.netlify.app by Prof Kam Tin Seong from SMU.


Hands On Exercise








Hands-on Exercise 1 - A Layered Grammar of Graphics: ggplot2 methods


THI THANH VAN TRAN APR 16, 2026







In-class Exercise








Analysing Superstore dataset


THI THANH VAN TRAN APR 18, 2026

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text: Learning Objectives:

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: 2.1 Installing and loading the required libraries
text:
2.1 Installing and loading the required libraries






Note



Note: Ensure that the pacman package has already been installed.


The code chunk below uses p_load() of pacman package to check if tidyverse packages are installed in the computer. If they are, then they will be launched into R. Otherwise, tidyverse will be installed and launched into R.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
2.2 Importing data

The code chunk below imports exam_data.csv into R environment by using read_csv() function of readr package.
readr is one of the tidyverse package.


exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

exam_data contains:

Year end examination grades of a cohort of primary 3 students from a local school.
There are a total of seven attributes. Four of them are categorical data type and the other three are in continuous data type.

The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE.
The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
3.1 R Graphics VS ggplot
First, let us compare how R Graphics (the core graphical functions of Base R) and ggplot plot a simple histogram.

R Graphicsggplot2



hist(exam_data$MATHS)











ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="black", 
                 fill="grey") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

















Note



Benefits of ggplot2 over in-built R graphics

Automated Legends: Unlike built-in plotting which requires manual legend creation, ggplot2 automatically generates legends based on the data’s aesthetics. This streamlines the process, especially for beginners who may find managing legends challenging.
Simplified Faceting: ggplot2 simplifies the creation of faceted plots (multiple subplots) compared to base R plotting. The latter can be quite complex, requiring setup with par() and potentially confusing for loops, which might be overwhelming for beginners.
Tidy Data Integration: ggplot2 encourages the use of ‘tidy data’ principles, aligning with how other R tools and packages handle data. This practice helps beginners learn a consistent and widely-adopted approach to data analysis in R.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
4.1 A Layered Grammar of Graphics
ggplot2 is an implementation of Leland Wilkinson’s Grammar of Graphics.
There are seven grammars of ggplot2:

Data: The dataset being plotted.
Aesthetics take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.
Geometrics: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
Facets split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Statistics, statiscal transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Coordinate systems define the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Themes modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.1 Geometric Objects: geom_bar
The code chunk below plots a bar chart by using geom_bar().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.2 Geometric Objects: geom_dotplot
In a dot plot, the width of a dot corresponds to the bin width (or maximum width, depending on the binning algorithm), and dots are stacked, with each dot representing one observation.
In the code chunk below, geom_dotplot() of ggplot2 is used to plot a dot plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)














Caution



The y scale is not very useful, in fact it is very misleading.








Note



The code chunk below performs the following two steps:

scale_y_continuous() is used to turn off the y-axis, and
binwidth argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5.




ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.3 Geometric Objects: geom_histogram()
In the code chunk below, geom_histogram() is used to create a simple histogram by using values in MATHS field of exam_data.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()














Note



Note that the default bin is 30.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: 7.4 Modifying a geometric object by changing geom()
text:
7.4 Modifying a geometric object by changing geom()
In the code chunk below,

bins argument is used to change the number of bins to 20,
fill argument is used to shade the histogram with light blue color, and
color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars in black


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: 7.5 Modifying a geometric object by changing aes()
text:
7.5 Modifying a geometric object by changing aes()
The code chunk below changes the interior colour of the histogram (i.e. fill) by using sub-group of aesthetic().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")














Note



This approach can be used to colour, fill and alpha of the geometric.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.6 Geometric Objects: geom-density()
geom-density() computes and plots kernel density estimate, which is a smoothed version of the histogram.
It is a useful alternative to the histogram for continuous data that comes from an underlying smooth distribution.
The code below plots the distribution of Maths scores in a kernel density estimate plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()








The code chunk below plots two kernel density lines by using colour or fill arguments of aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.7 Geometric Objects: geom_boxplot
geom_boxplot() displays continuous value list. It visualises five summary statistics (the median, two hinges and two whiskers), and all “outlying” points individually.
The code chunk below plots boxplots by using geom_boxplot().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()








Notches are used in box plots to help visually assess whether the medians of distributions differ. If the notches do not overlap, this is evidence that the medians are different.
The code chunk below plots the distribution of Maths scores by gender in notched plot instead of boxplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.8 Geometric Objects: geom_violin
geom_violin is designed for creating violin plot. Violin plots are a way of comparing multiple data distributions. With ordinary density curves, it is difficult to compare more than just a few distributions because the lines visually interfere with each other. With a violin plot, it’s easier to compare several distributions since they’re placed side by side.
The code below plot the distribution of Maths score by gender in violin plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.9 Geometric Objects: geom_point()
geom_point() is especially useful for creating scatterplot.
The code chunk below plots a scatterplot showing the Maths and English grades of pupils by using geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.10 geom objects can be combined
The code chunk below plots the data points on the boxplots by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
8.1 Working with stat()
The boxplots below are incomplete because the positions of the means were not shown.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: 8.2 Working with stat - the stat_summary() method
text:
8.2 Working with stat - the stat_summary() method
The code chunk below adds mean values by using stat_summary() function and overriding the default geom.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun.y="mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: 8.3 Working with stat - the geom() method
text:
8.3 Working with stat - the geom() method
The code chunk below adding mean values by using geom_() function and overriding the default stat.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun.y="mean",           
             colour ="red",          
             size=4)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: 8.4 Adding a best fit curve on a scatterplot?
text:
8.4 Adding a best fit curve on a scatterplot?
The scatterplot below shows the relationship of Maths and English grades of pupils. The interpretability of this graph can be improved by adding a best fit curve.
In the code chunk below, geom_smooth() is used to plot a best fit curve on the scatterplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=0.5)














Note



The default method used is loess.


The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
9.1 Working with facet_wrap()
facet_wrap wraps a 1d sequence of panels into 2d. This is generally a better use of screen space than facet_grid because most displays are roughly rectangular.
The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using facet-wrap().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
9.2 facet_grid() function
facet_grid() forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data.
The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using facet_grid().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
10.1 Working with Coordinate
By the default, the bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()








The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: 10.2 Changing the y- and x-axis range
text:
10.2 Changing the y- and x-axis range
The scatterplot on the right is slightly misleading because the y-aixs and x-axis range are not equal.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)








The code chunk below fixed both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
11.1 Working with theme
The code chunk below plot a horizontal bar chart using theme_gray().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_gray()








A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_classic().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()








A horizontal bar chart plotted using theme_minimal().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

title: In-class Exercise
text:
Note



Note: Ensure that the pacman package has already been installed.


The code chunk below uses p_load() of pacman package to check if required packages are installed. If they are, then they will be launched into R. Otherwise, they will be installed and launched into R.

pacman::p_load(readxl, tidyverse, ggplot2, scales, patchwork, lubridate)




The code chunk below imports Superstore.csv into R environment by using read_excel() function of readxl package.
Note: Despite the .csv extension, this is actually an Excel file format.

superstore <- read_excel("data/Superstore.csv")

title: In-class Exercise
text:
Note



Note: Ensure that the pacman package has already been installed.


The code chunk below uses p_load() of pacman package to check if required packages are installed. If they are, then they will be launched into R. Otherwise, they will be installed and launched into R.

pacman::p_load(readxl, tidyverse, ggplot2, scales, patchwork, lubridate)

title: In-class Exercise
text:
The code chunk below imports Superstore.csv into R environment by using read_excel() function of readxl package.
Note: Despite the .csv extension, this is actually an Excel file format.

superstore <- read_excel("data/Superstore.csv")

title: In-class Exercise
text:
2.1 Examining the Dataset Structure

# Display dataset dimensions
dim(superstore)

title: In-class Exercise
text:
2.2 Summary Statistics

# Summary of numerical variables
summary(superstore[, c("Sales", "Quantity", "Discount", "Profit")])

     Sales              Quantity         Discount          Profit

             0              0              0              0              0 
State/Province    Postal Code         Region     Product ID       Category 
             0              0              0              0              0 
  Sub-Category   Product Name          Sales       Quantity       Discount 
             0              0              0              0              0 
        Profit 
             0 








Important



Key Observations:

Dataset contains 10,194 transactions across 21 variables
Date range: 2022-01-03 to 2025-12-30 (4 years)
Average profit per transaction: $28.67
Discount ranges from 0% to 80%

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.1 Profitability Analysis by Category

# Calculate profit metrics by category
category_profit <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit),
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Count = n()
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Profit))

category_profit

# A tibble: 3 × 5
  Category        Total_Profit Avg_Profit Total_Sales Count
  <chr>                  <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> <int>
1 Technology           146543.      78.6      839893.  1865
2 Office Supplies      126023.      20.6      731893.  6128
3 Furniture             19730.       8.96     754748.  2201



3.1.1 Visualization: Profit by Category

ggplot(superstore, aes(x = reorder(Category, Profit, FUN = sum), y = Profit, fill = Category)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  stat_summary(fun = sum, geom = "point", shape = 23, size = 4, fill = "red") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Profit Distribution by Category",
    subtitle = "Red diamonds indicate total profit per category",
    x = "Category",
    y = "Profit per Transaction",
    caption = "Source: Superstore Dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    legend.position = "none"
  )














Tip



Finding: Technology products show the highest profitability with both higher median profit and total profit ($146,543), despite having fewer transactions than Office Supplies.

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.2 Sub-Category Performance Analysis

# Analyze profitability by sub-category
subcategory_profit <- superstore %>%
  group_by(`Sub-Category`) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit),
    Count = n()
  ) %>%
  arrange(Total_Profit)

# Display top and bottom performers
head(subcategory_profit, 5)

# A tibble: 5 × 4
  `Sub-Category` Total_Profit Avg_Profit Count
  <chr>                 <dbl>      <dbl> <int>
1 Tables              -17753.     -54.5    326
2 Bookcases            -3632.     -15.7    232
3 Supplies             -1171.      -6.10   192
4 Fasteners             2429.      10.6    229
5 Machines              3462.      29.6    117

tail(subcategory_profit, 5)

# A tibble: 5 × 4
  `Sub-Category` Total_Profit Avg_Profit Count
  <chr>                 <dbl>      <dbl> <int>
1 Binders              31426.       20.3  1548
2 Paper                34512.       24.9  1384
3 Accessories          41937.       54.1   775
4 Phones               45051.       49.9   903
5 Copiers              56094.      801.     70



3.2.1 Visualization: Sub-Category Profitability

ggplot(subcategory_profit, aes(x = reorder(`Sub-Category`, Total_Profit), y = Total_Profit)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = Total_Profit > 0), alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "#2ecc71", "FALSE" = "#e74c3c"),
    labels = c("Profitable", "Unprofitable")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Total Profit by Sub-Category",
    subtitle = "Tables, Bookcases, and Supplies show negative profitability",
    x = "Sub-Category",
    y = "Total Profit",
    fill = "Status",
    caption = "Source: Superstore Dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )














Warning



Critical Finding: Three sub-categories are unprofitable:

Tables: -$17,753 (worst performer)
Bookcases: -$3,632
Supplies: -$1,171

These require immediate strategic review!

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.3 The Discount Problem

# Analyze relationship between discount and profitability
discount_analysis <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Profit_Status = ifelse(Profit > 0, "Profitable", "Unprofitable"),
    Discount_Range = cut(Discount, 
                        breaks = c(0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0),
                        labels = c("0-10%", "11-20%", "21-30%", "31-50%", "50%+"),
                        include.lowest = TRUE)
  )

# Compare average discount
discount_comparison <- superstore %>%
  mutate(Profit_Status = ifelse(Profit > 0, "Profitable", "Unprofitable")) %>%
  group_by(Profit_Status) %>%
  summarise(
    Avg_Discount = mean(Discount),
    Count = n(),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit)
  )

discount_comparison

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  Profit_Status Avg_Discount Count Total_Profit
  <chr>                <dbl> <int>        <dbl>
1 Profitable          0.0804  8228      449336.
2 Unprofitable        0.469   1966     -157039.



3.3.1 Visualization: Discount vs Profitability

p1 <- ggplot(superstore, aes(x = Discount, y = Profit)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#3498db") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#e74c3c", linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Discount vs Profit Relationship",
    x = "Discount Rate",
    y = "Profit"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(discount_analysis, aes(x = Discount_Range, fill = Profit_Status)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Profitable" = "#2ecc71", "Unprofitable" = "#e74c3c")) +
  labs(
    title = "Profitability Rate by Discount Range",
    x = "Discount Range",
    y = "Percentage of Transactions",
    fill = "Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p1 + p2














Important



Major Insight:

Profitable orders average 8% discount
Unprofitable orders average 48% discount
Clear negative correlation: higher discounts = lower profitability
Transactions with 50%+ discount are predominantly unprofitable

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.4 Regional Performance

# Analyze performance by region
regional_performance <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Region) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit),
    Count = n(),
    Profit_Margin = (Total_Profit / Total_Sales) * 100
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Profit))

regional_performance

# A tibble: 4 × 6
  Region  Total_Sales Total_Profit Avg_Profit Count Profit_Margin
  <chr>         <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> <int>         <dbl>
1 West        739814.      110799.       34.1  3253         15.0 
2 East        691828.       94883.       31.8  2986         13.7 
3 South       391722.       46749.       28.9  1620         11.9 
4 Central     503171.       39865.       17.1  2335          7.92



3.4.1 Visualization: Regional Analysis

p3 <- ggplot(regional_performance, aes(x = reorder(Region, Total_Profit), y = Total_Profit, fill = Region)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(Total_Profit, accuracy = 1)), 
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(), limits = c(0, 120000)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Total Profit by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Total Profit"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p4 <- ggplot(regional_performance, aes(x = reorder(Region, Avg_Profit), y = Avg_Profit, fill = Region)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(Avg_Profit, accuracy = 0.1)), 
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(), limits = c(0, 40)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Profit per Transaction by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Average Profit"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p3 + p4

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.5 Temporal Analysis

# Prepare data for time series analysis
monthly_trends <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Year = year(`Order Date`),
    Month = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month")
  ) %>%
  group_by(Month, Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Calculate overall monthly metrics
monthly_overall <- superstore %>%
  mutate(Month = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month")) %>%
  group_by(Month) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Profit_Margin = (Total_Profit / Total_Sales) * 100
  )


3.5.1 Visualization: Sales and Profit Trends

p5 <- ggplot(monthly_trends, aes(x = Month, y = Total_Sales, color = Category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Sales Trend by Category",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Total Sales",
    color = "Category"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p6 <- ggplot(monthly_overall, aes(x = Month, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c3e50", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Profit Margin Trend",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p5 / p6

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.6 Customer Segment Analysis

# Analyze by customer segment
segment_analysis <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Segment) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit),
    Count = n(),
    Avg_Discount = mean(Discount)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Profit))

segment_analysis

# A tibble: 3 × 6
  Segment     Total_Sales Total_Profit Avg_Profit Count Avg_Discount
  <chr>             <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> <int>        <dbl>
1 Consumer       1170660.      136371.       25.8  5281        0.158
2 Corporate       715806.       94250.       30.5  3090        0.156
3 Home Office     440068.       61676.       33.8  1823        0.147



3.6.1 Visualization: Segment Performance

ggplot(superstore, aes(x = Sales, y = Profit, color = Segment)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.5) +
  facet_wrap(~Segment) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  labs(
    title = "Sales vs Profit by Customer Segment",
    subtitle = "Relationship between sales and profit varies across segments",
    x = "Sales Amount",
    y = "Profit",
    color = "Segment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    legend.position = "none"
  )

title: In-class Exercise
text:
4.1 Summary of Critical Findings

# Create summary table of key metrics
key_metrics <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total Transactions",
    "Total Sales",
    "Total Profit",
    "Overall Profit Margin",
    "Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Loss from Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Most Profitable Category",
    "Least Profitable Sub-Category"
  ),
  Value = c(
    format(nrow(superstore), big.mark = ","),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Sales)),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit)),
    paste0(round((sum(superstore$Profit) / sum(superstore$Sales)) * 100, 2), "%"),
    paste0(format(sum(superstore$Profit < 0), big.mark = ","), 
           " (", round(mean(superstore$Profit < 0) * 100, 1), "%)"),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit[superstore$Profit < 0])),
    "Technology",
    "Tables"
  )
)

knitr::kable(key_metrics, caption = "Superstore Performance Summary")


Superstore Performance Summary


Metric
Value




Total Transactions
10,194


Total Sales
$2,326,534


Total Profit
$292,297


Overall Profit Margin
12.56%


Unprofitable Transactions
1,901 (18.6%)


Loss from Unprofitable Transactions
-$157,039


Most Profitable Category
Technology


Least Profitable Sub-Category
Tables











NoteBusiness Recommendations




Discount Strategy Review

Implement discount caps (max 30%) especially for Furniture
High discounts (50%+) are destroying profitability
Average profitable discount: 8% vs unprofitable: 48%

Product Line Optimization

Consider discontinuing or repricing Tables (-$17,753 loss)
Review Bookcases and Supplies categories
Expand Copiers and Phones product lines (highest profit margins)

Regional Focus

West region is the strongest performer ($110,799 profit)
Central region needs strategic intervention (lowest avg profit)
Consider targeted marketing in underperforming regions

Segment Strategy

Home Office segment has highest profit per transaction ($33.83)
Consumer segment provides volume; optimize for margin improvement
Corporate segment shows strong potential for growth
